activity_metadata.objective: Students review key points from "Teenagers" by Pat Mora to show literal comprehension of the text.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the Quiz and Think question segment of the Teenagers First Read activity. It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through demonstrating comprehension by responding to questions using evidence from the text.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Teenagers by Pat Mora

(1) One day they disappear

       into their rooms.

       Doors and lips shut

       and we become strangers

(5) in our own home.

       I pace the hall, hear whispers,

       a code I knew but can't remember,

       mouthed by mouths I taught to speak.

       Years later the door opens.

(10) I see faces I once held,

       open as sunflowers in my hands. I see

       familiar skin now stretched on long bodies

       that move past me

       glowing almost like pearls.
exit_ticket: Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

questions_map.question:
**Voice** is the way an author uses word choice, tone, and speech patterns to show the personality of a speaker, narrator, or character. Voice gives the sense that a real person is talking to the reader or to other characters. 

Whose voice is telling us about the teenagers?
In lines 4 and 5, the speaker says that ”we become strangers in our own home.” Who are the strangers in this home?
Re-read lines 6-8. Which of the following inferences is best supported by these lines?
Look at lines 6-8 again. What does the word “code” mean in this context?
Re-read lines 9-14. These lines primarily suggest that _____.
Write two or three sentences that explain what happens to the “strangers” when “years later, the door opens”?

responses.content: Teenagers were talking to a speaker

Focus on who is describing the teenagers and what their connection might be. Who would say "mouths I taught to speak" or "faces I once held"? 🤔

USER_15.question: Re-read lines 6-8. Which of the following inferences is best supported by these lines?

responses.content: In lines 4 and 5, the speaker says that ”we become strangers in our own home.” Who are the strangers in this home?

You said the strangers are the teenagers. The word "we" in line 5 tells us that it's not just the teenagers—the parents are also strangers in this situation.

Look back at line 5: "we become strangers in our own home." Who is saying this line, and what does the word "we" include? Who else becomes a stranger along with the teenagers? 🤔

Go to line 5: "we become strangers in our own home." The speaker is a parent who uses the word "we." Look at who is speaking this line—if a parent says "we become strangers," who does "we" include? Does it mean only the teenagers, or is the parent also part of "we"? 🤔

responses.content: Teenagers were talking to a speaker.

The teenagers are being described by someone else. The speaker is observing them and talking about what happened over the years.

Focus on who is describing the teenagers and what their connection might be. Who would say "mouths I taught to speak" or "faces I once held"?

You pointed out that the teenagers are being described by someone else who watched them grow up. The question asks whose voice is telling us about the teenagers, so you'll need to identify who the speaker is. 

Consider the clues you noticed. If someone taught the teenagers to speak, held their faces as babies, and now paces the hall of "our home," what relationship does this person have to the teenagers? 🤔
